title: Local leader-follower cover/undercut features, not global pit-wave counts
summary: Game-theoretic F1 strategy work emphasizes asymmetric leader/follower decisions rather than symmetric field averages.
rationale: Game-theoretic F1 strategy work emphasizes asymmetric leader/follower decisions rather than symmetric field averages. That suggests the next opponent-aware test should focus on the nearest relevant cars ahead/behind on the same lap, with separate cover and undercut pressures, instead of only aggregate pit-wave features.
implementation_hint: For each row, derive nearest one or two cars ahead and behind within the same Year/Race/LapNumber, then create asymmetric features: ahead-pitted-cover flag, behind-fresher-undercut threat, same-compound nearest-rival age gap, and position-conditioned pressure scores. Blend this into the existing tree stack rather than building a separate model family.
expected_effect: Could recover signal missed by global field summaries, especially for front-runners and tightly packed midfield strategy battles.
risk: If gaps or pace context are too weak in the available columns, these features may just add noisy local comparisons; keep the set sparse.
